coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dot/silver nanocomposite and its application for colorimetric detection of methimazole
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Hallaj, Tooba
Asadollahi, Hamideh
Song, Zhenlun
de Frutos, Marta
Hildebrandt, Niko
coredata.dc:description: Here we present a very simple and fast method for preparation of carbon quantum dot/silver (CQD/Ag) nanocomposites at room temperature. Glucose-derived CQDs were prepared by a facile microwave-assisted method and used as both reducing and stabilizing agents for synthesis of CQD/Ag nanocomposites. We found that a unique interaction between as-prepared nanocomposites and anti-thyroid drug methimazole occurs in the solution, which results in a sharp color change from yellow to red. At the same time, the intensity of surface plasmon resonance peak of CQD/Ag nanocomposites at 400nm decreases and a new peak appears at higher wavelengths. This finding formed a basis for developing a new colorimetric detection method for methimazole. The calibration curve for this drug was linear in the concentration range of 2.0–40μgL−1 with a detection limit of 1.0μgL−1. The method was applied to the determination of methimazole in urine samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot/Ag nanocomposite
Silver nanoparticles
C-dots
Colorimetric detection
Methimazole

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for determination of methimazole
Effect of methimazole on the CQD/Ag nanocomposites
Optimization of conditions for methimazole determination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of principle of the methimazole assay.
(a) TEM image of CQDs and (b) HRTEM image of CQD/Ag nanocomposites.
UV-vis absorption spectrum of (a) CQDs and (b) CQD/Ag nanocomposites.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of CQD/Ag nanocomposite.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) TEM image of CQD/Ag nanocomposites in the presence of methimazole.

Optimization of conditions: (a) Effect of pH value, CQD/Ag (2.0mL), buffer (4.8mM), time (30min), methimazole (10μgL–1); (b) Effect of buffer concentration, pH=4.0, other conditions are as in a (c) Effect of CQD/Ag solution volume, buffer 4.8mM, other conditions are as in b; (d) Effect of time, CQD/Ag 2.5mL, other conditions are as in c.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQD/Ag nanocomposites in the presence of methimazole (2.0–40μgL−1) and (b) corresponding calibration curve. Conditions: CQD/Ag (2.5mL), Briton-Robinson buffer (4.8mM, pH=4.0), time (15min), wavelength 400nm.
Comparison of the introduced method for preparation of CQD/Ag nanocomposite with some previously published methods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of methimazole in human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dot/silver nanocomposite and its application for colorimetric detection of methimazole

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Surface Protection Research Group, Surface Department, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 519 Zhuangshi Road, Ningbo, 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NanoBioPhotonics (nanofret.com), Institute for Integrative Biology of the Cell (I2BC), Université Paris-Saclay, Université Paris-Sud, CNRS, CEA, Orsay, France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Integrative Biology of the Cell (I2BC)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQD/Ag nanocomposites were synthesized by a simple method using glucose-derived CQDs and then applied for colorimetric detection of methimazole.

Here we present a very simple and fast method for preparation of carbon quantum dot/silver (CQD/Ag) nanocomposites at room temperature. Glucose-derived CQDs were prepared by a facile microwave-assisted method and used as both reducing and stabilizing agents for synthesis of CQD/Ag nanocomposites. We found that a unique interaction between as-prepared nanocomposites and anti-thyroid drug methimazole occurs in the solution, which results in a sharp color change from yellow to red. At the same time, the intensity of surface plasmon resonance peak of CQD/Ag nanocomposites at 400nm decreases and a new peak appears at higher wavelengths. This finding formed a basis for developing a new colorimetric detection method for methimazole. The calibration curve for this drug was linear in the concentration range of 2.0–40μgL−1 with a detection limit of 1.0μgL−1. The method was applied to the determination of methimazole in urine samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have attracted great interest due to their unique and tunable optical properties originating from local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR). Spherical AgNPs in 10-nm size range have a strong LSPR absorption band with a maximum around 390nm [1]. The position of this peak depends on the size and shape of NPs and their local dielectric environment. As the NP size increases, the LSPR peak is red-shifted due to the electromagnetic retardation in larger particles [2,3]. Such LSPR-based optical phenomena have served as a platform for developing several interesting sensors and biosensors [4–7]. At the same time, various materials have been used as reducing agents and stabilizers for preparation of AgNPs. Among them, carbon-based nanomaterials are very interesting and have attracted tremendous attention in the past few years [8–13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10nm which exhibit interesting physicochemical properties such as size- and wavelength-dependent photoluminescence, resistance to photobleaching and ease of bioconjugation. These outstanding characteristics of CQDs make them promising in various fields [14–23]. Recently CQDs have been used as reducing agents for synthesis of metal nanoparticles for sensing applications. For example, Wang et al. [8] exploited carbon dots for preparation of Ag@Carbon dot composites and determined glucose during the synthesis process. Shen et al. [10] also applied CQDs as reducing agents for the growth of AgNPs and developed an optical probe for assay of biothiols using the prepared NPs. Chen and coworkers [24] reported a new approach for in situ growth of AgNPs on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and exploited them for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose. Moreover, Shi et al. [25] used nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) as a reducing agent and stabilizer for the formation of N-GQDs/AgNPs hybrid and developed a colorimetric method for discrimination of dihydroxybenzene isomers. Despite various advantages of these methods, the synthesis procedures in most of them need high temperature and/or long reaction time.

Methimazole is one of the major drugs for treatment of hyperthyreosis. It is a thioamide that inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis by blocking peroxidase-catalyzed reactions and iodination of the tyrosine residues of thyroglobulin [26]. It has been reported that methimazole may also cause dose-related side effects including cutaneous reactions, arthralgia, gastrointestinal upset and agranulocytosis [27]. Therefore, determination of methimazole in biological fluids is important and various analytical methods including chromatography [28,29], electrochemistry [30–32], spectrophotometry [33,34], fluorescence [35,36] and chemiluminescence [37,38] have been reported for this purpose.

Herein, we report on a facile synthesis of CQD/Ag nanocomposites using glucose-derived CQDs as both reducing and stabilizing agents. These nanocomposites showed a unique color change from yellow to red in response to trace amounts of methimazole. Based on this phenomenon, we have established a novel colorimetric method for the determination of methimazole in urine samples.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Cary-100 Spectrophotometer (Varian; www.varianinc.com) using 1.0-cm standard quartz cells. Fluorescence spectra were recorded by RF-540 spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Tensor 27, Bruker). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured by a Siemens D 500 instrument (Germany). The size and shape of CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, EM 900, Zeiss, Germany). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were carried out using a JEOL 2010 UHR microscope operating at 200keV. Images were collected using a Gatan Orius SC200 CCD camera. Samples were prepared by depositing a 4μL drop of CQD/Ag suspension on commercial carbon TEM grids. XPS spectra were recorded by a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray and a multichannel detector. Raman spectra were recorded using a micro-Raman spectrometer (Renishaw in Via Reflex), using a Nd: YAG laser with a wavelength of 532nm equipped with a CCD detector at room temperature.

All reagents were of analytical-reagent grade. Doubly distilled deionized water (obtained from Ghazi Serum Co. Tabriz, Iran) was used throughout the experiment. Glucose, Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and NH3 were all purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Pure methimazole was kindly provided by Loghman Company (Tehran, Iran).

CQDs were synthesized from glucose by a microwave-assisted method [39]. Briefly, 6.0mL of 11% aqueous solution of glucose was put into a glass bottle with a cover and heated in a conventional microwave oven at 720W for 10min. During this process the color of the residue changes to yellow which implies the formation of CQDs. The volume of obtained CQDs was adjusted to 6.0mL with deionized water and purified by dialysis against deionized water for 5h (the cutoff of the dialysis membrane was equivalent to MW=2000Da).

CQD/Ag nanocomposites were prepared by chemical reduction of AgNO3 with CQDs at room temperature. 3.0mL of the prepared CQD solution, 1.0mL of 0.01M AgNO3 and 0.5mL of 0.0625M NH3 were added into a 50-mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with deionized water. The reaction mixture was incubated at room temperature for 10min. The obtained yellow solution of CQD/Ag nanocomposites was stable for more than one month in the refrigerator at 4°C (Fig. S1, Electronic Supplementary Materials, ESM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for determination of methimazole
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 2.5mL of CQD/Ag nanocomposites and 200μL of 0.12M Britton–Robinson buffer solution (pH 4.0) were added into a 5.0-mL volumetric flask. Then, an appropriate amount of methimazole standard or sample solution was added and final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 5.0mL with deionized water. After incubation for 15min at room temperature, the absorbance was measured at 400nm.

Drug-free human urine samples were obtained from volunteers and spiked by adding suitable concentrations of standard methimazole. No special pretreatment was necessary for samples. 50μL of each sample was taken for analysis according to the general procedure.

In this work, a simple microwave-assisted method was used for synthesis of CQDs from glucose as a carbon source [39,40]. During the microwave heating, glucose is pyrolyzed and then converted to CQDs. TEM image of the CQDs, shown in Fig. 1 a, indicates that the prepared CQDs are fairly uniform and their sizes are below 10nm. UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 2 a) exhibits two distinct absorption peaks at 226 and 281nm, which are attributed to the π–π* transition of CC and the n–π* transition of CO, respectively [14]. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs are shown in Fig. S2 (ESM). As can be seen, the prepared CQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent fluorescence and maximum emission (∼455nm) is obtained with an excitation wavelength of 400nm.

We used the synthesized CQDs as reducing and stabilizing agents for preparation of stable CQD/Ag nanocomposites. According to the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3, ESM), there are some functional groups on the surface of CQDs such as hydroxyl, which possess reducing activity. Therefore, CQDs can serve as the electron donors in the reaction with electron-withdrawing agents. Moreover, FT-IR spectrum confirms the presence of carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs which have a strong affinity for Ag+ ions. So these ions are probably adsorbed on the surface of CQDs via carboxyl groups. Hence, CQDs can act as stabilizing agents to prevent the aggregation of the produced nanocomposites [8,10,12,25]. In order to confirm the formation of CQD/Ag nanocomposites, XRD pattern, EDS, XPS and Raman spectra of the as-prepared nanocomposites were recorded. According to EDS spectrum (Fig. S4, ESM), the carbon and Ag content of CQD/Ag nanocomposites is about 60.0 and 1.0 A%, respectively. The XPS spectrum shows C1s, Ag 3d and O1s peaks at 284, 367 and 531eV, respectively (Fig. 3 a). As demonstrated by high-resolution XPS of C1s (Fig. 3Ac), the as-prepared nanocomposites contain CO, CC and CO groups. Additionally, the XPS peaks which appeared at 371eV and 365eV can be attributed to the binding energies of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2, respectively (Fig. 3b). These results confirm that AgNPs are decorated on the surface of CQDs. The carbon and Ag content of nanocomposite according to XPS was found to be 57 and 0.26 At%. The XRD pattern of nanocomposites is also shown in Fig. 4 a. The peaks appeared at 2θ=38°, 44°, 65°, and 78° are characteristic peaks of crystalline (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of Ag, respectively, which suggest the formation of crystalline AgNPs on the surface of CQDs. In addition, the typical (002) peak of CQDs can be observed around 2θ=17°. This peak is very weak and hardly distinguishable, which is due to the formation of AgNPs on the surface of CQDs. Moreover, compared to the XRD pattern of CQDs, which exhibits a broad (002) peak around 19°, it has shifted to lower angle. This phenomenon may be attributed to an increase in the interlayer spacing of CQDs as a result of the crystal growth of AgNPs [25,41]. The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of as-prepared nanocomposites (Fig. 1b) shows that they are spherical NPs with sizes in the range of 14±3nm. In addition, the HRTEM images reveal that the interplanar spacings for the two clear lattice fringes are 0.23nm and 0.20nm, corresponding to the (111) and (200) lattice planes of Ag, respectively.

Raman spectrum of the as-prepared CQDs/Ag composites is shown in Fig. 4b. Two peaks were observed at 1358 and 1590cm−1, which are the typical D and G bands of carbon nanomaterials, respectively. It should be mentioned that the spectrum of CQDs did not show any Raman signal probably due to the strong fluorescence background. But in the composite the fluorescence of CQDs was partly quenched by Ag nanoparticles, indicating the successful synthesis of the CQDs/Ag composites.
Atomic absorption spectrometric measurement shows that final Ag content of nanocomposite is about 1.85×10−4 M which is close to initial concentration.
UV–vis absorption spectrum of the nanocomposites was also recorded (Fig. 2b), which exhibits a surface plasmon resonance peak at 400nm.

The conditions for preparation of CQD/Ag nanocomposites were optimized in terms of the amount of CQDs, AgNO3 and NH3 and the reaction time. The respective data are given in the Electronic Supplementary Material (Fig. S5). The relative amount of CQDs and Ag+ ions is the most important factor. When the ratio of CQD/Ag+ is too low, the formed nanoparticles are not stable and tend to aggregate. On the other hand, when this ratio is too high, the formation of nanocomposites would be suppressed probably because of binding of more and more Ag+ ions to CQDs. Compared to other reported methods for preparation of CQD/Ag nanocomposites, which require high temperature, long reaction time and/or additional reducing agents, our introduced procedure is simpler and faster and requires only mild conditions (Table 1 ). In addition, the synthesis procedure of glucose-derived CQDs is much simpler than other methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of methimazole on the CQD/Ag nanocomposites
Optimization of conditions for methimazole determination
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the maximum sensitivity for determination of methimazole by CQD/Ag nanocomposites, several experimental variables such as pH, concentration of buffer, amount of nanocomposite and reaction time were optimized. The decreased absorption intensity (ΔA=A0−A, where A0 and A represent the absorbance of nanocomposites at 400nm in the absence and presence of methimazole, respectively) was taken as the dependent variable (response) for optimization.

The influence of pH was studied in the range of 2.0–7.0 using Britton-Robinson buffer. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the response increases with pH, and reaches a maximum around pH 4.0. At lower pH values, the absorbance of CQD/Ag nanocomposites decreases due to the instability of nanocomposites in acidic conditions. Furthermore, the pH of solution should be appropriate for interaction of methimazole with nanocomposites because the ionization of methimazole is dependent on the pH. It seems that these effects reach a compromise at pH 4.0.

The influence of buffer concentration varying from 1.2×10−3 to 1.2×10−2 M was also studied. As seen from Fig. 6b, the response increases with increasing buffer concentration up to 4.8×10−3 M. At higher buffer concentrations, the absorbance signal in absence of methimazole decreases due to the aggregation of CQD/Ag nanocomposites.

The effect of amount of CQD/Ag nanocomposites on the decreased absorption intensity was also examined in the range of 1.0–4.0mL. According to Fig. 6c the maximum response is obtained with 2.5mL of CQD/Ag nanocomposites. Therefore, this amount was selected as the optimum value for subsequent experiments.

Finally, the response over the time after addition of methimazole was investigated. As seen from Fig. 6d, a 15-min incubation time is required to complete the interaction between CQD/Ag nanocomposites and methimazole.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, the effect of some common inorganic ions and organic compounds on the determination of 10μgL−1 methimazole was investigated. The tolerable mass ratios for interferences in relative error of <5% are summarized in Table 2 . Most of cations and anions do not interfere with methimazole determination, except Cu2+ and Fe3+, which probably can interact with CQDs [14]. Tested amino acids and some other organic compounds also do not interfere significantly, except cysteine, which has sulfur group and can bind to CQD/Ag nanocomposite. However, comparison of the obtained tolerance limits with normal concentrations of common species in urine (Table S2, ESM) shows that the final concentration of these species after dilution of samples during measurement step will be lower than their tolerance limits. Therefore, it can be concluded that our method possesses a good selectivity for analysis of urine samples.

The developed method was applied to the determination of methimazole in spiked urine samples. The obtained results are summarized in Table 3 and corresponding UV–vis spectra are given in Fig. S6 (ESM). Statistical analysis of these results using Student t-test confirmed that there are no significant differences between added and found values. Moreover, the given spectra for real samples show that there is no obvious interference from urine matrix.

We demonstrated that glucose-derived CQDs, prepared by microwave-assisted method, can be exploited for facile synthesis of CQD/Ag nanocomposites. Compared to previously reported methods, which need high temperature and/or long reaction time, our method is very simple and fast and does not need high temperature. In addition, it was found that in the presence of trace amounts of methimazole, the intensity of plasmon absorption of the prepared CQD/Ag nanocomposites remarkably decreased and a color change occurred. On the basis of this phenomenon, we designed a simple and sensitive colorimetric method for the determination of methimazole in urine samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.01.003.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Noble metals on the nanoscale: optical and photothermal properties and some applications in imaging, sensing, biology, and medicine
Shape-controlled synthesis of silver nanoparticles for plasmonic and sensing applications
The optical properties of metal nanoparticles: the influence of size, shape, and dielectric environment
Microfluidic chip-based silver nanoparticles aptasensor for colorimetric detection of thrombin
Selective colorimetric sensing of cysteine in aqueous solutions using silver nanoparticles in the presence of Cr3+
Analyte-induced photoreduction method for visual and colorimetric detection of tyrosine
Colorimetric detection of kanamycin based on analyte-protected silver nanoparticles and aptamer-selective sensing mechanism
Accelerated reducing synthesis of Ag@CDs composite and simultaneous determination of glucose during the synthetic process
Carbon dot reduced bimetallic nanoparticles: size and surface plasmon resonance tunability for enhanced catalytic applications
Assay of biothiols by regulating the growth of silver nanoparticles with C-dots as reducing agent
Versatile surface plasmon resonance of carbon-dot-supported silver nanoparticles in polymer optoelectronic devices
Reduced carbon dots employed for synthesizing metal nanoclusters and nanoparticles
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as a reducing agent to synthesize Ag nanoparticles for non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide detection
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
A green solid-phase method for preparation of carbon nitride quantum dots and their applications in chemiluminescent dopamine sensing
Eco-friendly and rapid microwave synthesis of green fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots for vitro bioimaging
Solid-state synthesis of self-functional carbon quantum dots for detection of bacteria and tumor cells
Fluorescence turn-on sensing of ascorbic acid and alkaline phosphatase activity based on graphene quantum dots
Electrical transport in transverse direction through silicon carbon alloy multilayers containing regular size silicon quantum dots
Preparation of carbon quantum dots with a high quantum yield and the application in labeling bovine serum albumin
Green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse pulp
A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ by using amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe
Rapid and simple nanosensor by combination of graphene quantum dots and enzymatic inhibition mechanisms
In situ growth of silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Visual discrimination of dihydroxybenzene isomers based on a nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot-silver nanoparticle hybrid
Analysis of antithyroid drugs in surface water by using liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry
Determination of methimazole in urine by liquid chromatography
Potentiometric and voltammetric determination of methimazole
Electrochemical determination of methimazole based on the acetylene black/chitosan film electrode and its application to rat serum samples
Electrochemistry of methimazole on fluorine-doped tin oxide electrodes and its square-wave voltammetric determination in pharmaceutical formulations
Spectrophotometric determination of methimazole D-penicillamine, captopril, and disulfiram in pure form and drug formulations
Spectrophotometric determination of methimazole in pharmaceutical, serum and urine samples by reaction with potassium ferricyanide-Fe(III)
Sensitive turn-on fluorescence assay of methimazole based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between acridine orange and silver nanoparticles
A novel method for methimazole determination using CdSe quantum dots as fluorescence probes
Determination of methimazole and carbimazole by flow-injection with chemiluminescence detection based on the inhibition of the Cu(II)-catalysed luminol–hydrogen peroxide reaction
A novel method for sensing of methimazole using gold nanoparticle-catalyzed chemiluminescent reaction
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Dramatic enhancement effect of carbon quantum dots on the chemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+–Ce(IV) reaction and application to the determination of 4-nitrophenol
Green synthesis of silver nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped graphene for hydrogen peroxide detection
Microwave-assisted rapid synthesis of Ag nanoparticles/graphene nanosheet composites and their application for hydrogen peroxide detection
A label-free and turn-on fluorescence sensor for sensitive and selective detection of iodide using carbon nanodots/silver nanocomposites
Electrochemiluminescence of a nanoAg/carbon nanodot composite and its application to detect sulfide ions
Facile synthesis of core–shell–satellite Ag/C/Ag nanocomposites using carbon nanodots as reductant and their SERS properties

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mohammad Amjadi is professor of Analytical Chemistry at the University of Tabriz. His current research interest includes the use of carbon nanomaterials, quantum dots and plasmonic nanoparticles to design new optical detection strategies for pharmaceutical, biological and environmental applications. He is also interested in development of fluorescence and chemiluminescence-based analytical methods.

Tooba Hallaj is currently a Postdoc researcher at the University of Tabriz. She obtained her PhD on Analytical Chemistry in 2014. Her current studies have focused on the applications of carbon nanomaterials in chemiluminescence, fluorescence and colorimetric sensing.
Hamideh Asadollahi obtained her MSc in Analytical Chemistry in 2015. She worked on the synthesis and analytical applications of carbon quantum dots and silver nanaoparticles.

Zhenlun Song, Ph.D, Professor, obtained his Doctor of Science degree in Materials process engineering from Southeast University (1998, Nanjing, China). After postdoctoral position at Nanjing University, JSPS fellow at National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS), Tsukuba (Japan), research associate at University of Southern California, Los Angeles, USA, he took up an academic appointment in Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, where he is currently Professor of materials science and engineering. His research interest includes the processing and fabrication of high-performance metals (i.e. biometals), biosensors, electrocatalysis, electrochemical synthesis of nanocomposites, properties and microstructural development of materials during deformation, corrosion and protection of metals, and metallic oxide for energy conversion. He is the author of over 90 publications in these and other areas.

Marta de Frutos is a researcher at the Laboratoire de Physique des Solides (Orsay, France). Her research focuses on the biophysical area. She has an extensive experience in analytical approaches based on electron microscopy for the structural and chemical characterization at the nanometer and sub-nanometer scale of biomaterials, nanomaterials, biological and medical systems.

Niko Hildebrandt is full professor at Université Paris-Sud, where he is leading the group of NanoBioPhotonics (nanofret.com) at the Institute for Integrative Biology of the Cell (I2BC) with a research focus on time-resolved FRET spectroscopy and imaging for nanobioanalysis.
